title: The Almanack of MM — Mental Model Quiz: Part II Saving Yourself
source: The Almanack of MM by Eric Jorgenson (2020)

questions.question: MM says emulating others is a 'fool's errand.' What is the underlying logical argument he makes, and why does being yourself give you a structural competitive advantage no one else can match?
questions.short_answer: The combinatorics of human DNA and experience make every person genuinely unique and irreplaceable. No one can out-compete you at being you because no one else has your exact combination of genes, experiences, and desires.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's argument is rooted in combinatorics: the sheer mathematical improbability of two humans sharing identical DNA and developmental histories means everyone is fundamentally differentiated. When you try to copy someone else, you are competing against their original, and you will always lose that asymmetric race. But when you operate as yourself, you occupy a niche of one — a natural monopoly. The implication is that your goal should not be to build decision frameworks modeled on other people but to find the project, business, or art that specifically needs your unique configuration. The competitive edge comes from radical authenticity, not from convergence toward someone else's model.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Be Yourself

questions.question: MM's greatest mentor he never met told him 'Be yourself, with passionate intensity.' Why does the intensity modifier matter, and what does MM mean by needing to be 'irrationally obsessed' to make an original contribution?
questions.short_answer: Passionate intensity is what allows a unique self to cut through noise and actually produce something — without it, authenticity alone is inert. Irrational obsession signals that the motivation is internal rather than driven by external approval or rational cost-benefit analysis.
questions.elaborated_answer: Being yourself is a necessary but insufficient condition for making an original contribution. The world is filled with people being themselves passively; the differentiator is the force of conviction behind it. 'Irrational' obsession is MM's way of pointing out that if you are doing something because it passes a rational external test, others have already done that calculation and competed you out. The person who succeeds at something genuinely new is usually the one whose drive cannot be explained by conventional reasoning — they are pulled forward by an internal signal so strong it overrides social skepticism. Passionate intensity is also what sustains the long, unrewarded periods before an original contribution is recognized.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Be Yourself

questions.question: MM warns against building 'checklists and decision frameworks built on what other people are doing.' What is the hidden cost of using others' frameworks to make life decisions?
questions.short_answer: Borrowed frameworks optimize for someone else's goals and circumstances, not your own. Following them guarantees you arrive at someone else's destination — even if you execute perfectly.
questions.elaborated_answer: Decision frameworks are encoding a particular worldview, set of values, and starting conditions. When MM's path is distilled into a checklist, it implicitly assumes you share his context, desires, and risk profile. Applying it to your life introduces a systematic error from the first step. More subtly, constantly consulting external frameworks drowns out the inner voice that carries your specific knowledge — the signal unique to you. MM frames it as a form of self-abandonment: you stop listening to what you actually want in exchange for the false security of following a proven path. The practical result is that you work hard, execute well, and end up living a life that was never yours to begin with.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Be Yourself

questions.question: MM ranks his own physical health above his happiness, his family, and his work. Why is this ordering counterintuitive, and what is the logical structure that makes it defensible?
questions.short_answer: Health is foundational infrastructure: without it, every other priority degrades or collapses. Placing health first is not selfishness but a prerequisite for reliably delivering on every other commitment.
questions.elaborated_answer: Most people intuitively place family or work first because those involve immediate obligations and social pressure. MM's inversion recognizes that health is the platform everything else runs on — a sick or physically degraded person cannot perform as a parent, partner, or professional at full capacity. Treating health as the top priority also forces a brutal prioritization discipline: if something cannot wait for a thirty-minute workout, it has revealed itself as a true emergency, not merely an urgent-feeling distraction. Additionally, MM notes that health problems sharply clarify what matters in life, implying that most day-to-day 'priorities' are revealed as trivial the moment health is threatened. The ordering is therefore an act of long-term rationality, not self-indulgence.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Care for Yourself

questions.question: MM uses the sugar-fat interaction to illustrate a broader principle about diet. What is the specific mechanism he describes, and what general lesson does it carry about how evolved systems behave in modern environments?
questions.short_answer: Sugar signals scarcity and drives hunger even when calories are plentiful, while fat drives satiety — but the sugar signal dominates, causing overeating when the two are combined. This shows that evolved drives misfire badly when exposed to stimuli that didn't exist in their evolutionary environment.
questions.elaborated_answer: In nature, high concentrations of sugar and fat rarely occur together, so the body never evolved a shutoff mechanism for their combination. Sugar triggers a 'rare resource — consume now' alarm because historically it signaled ripe fruit. Fat triggers fullness. When you combine them, as in nearly every modern dessert and processed food, you get maximum hunger drive with maximum caloric density — the worst possible outcome for appetite regulation. The deeper principle MM draws is that human bodies evolved for scarcity but now live in abundance, and the old signals are hacked by the modern food environment. This is a general model: any evolved system exposed to a supernormal stimulus for which it has no calibrated response will be systematically exploited. The same logic applies to social media's exploitation of status drives and pornography's exploitation of reproductive instincts.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Care for Yourself

questions.question: MM says 'I don't have time' is just another way of saying 'it's not a priority.' What is the hidden decision this reframe forces, and how does it change the structure of commitment?
questions.short_answer: The reframe strips the excuse of its emotional neutrality and makes the trade-off explicit: you are choosing not to prioritize something, not being prevented by external circumstances. This forces honest ownership of your choices.
questions.elaborated_answer: Saying 'I don't have time' is a passive construction that locates the problem outside the self, in the impersonal flow of hours. MM's reframe re-locates it: time is allocated by priority, and if the workout didn't happen, the real statement is 'the workout ranked below other things I did today.' This is harder to sustain as a long-term position because it requires explicitly endorsing the lower priority of health. The mechanism MM uses to resolve this is to declare his health the single top priority — once that is established, the logic is airtight: no other thing can legitimately claim that slot, so the workout always happens first. It also explains why fuzzy baskets of ten to fifteen equal priorities produce nothing: without a strict ordering, every item can always be displaced by every other item.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Care for Yourself

questions.question: MM invokes Jerzy Gregorek's maxim 'Easy choices, hard life. Hard choices, easy life.' How does this principle apply across domains beyond diet and exercise, and what does it reveal about delayed versus immediate feedback systems?
questions.short_answer: Easy choices feel costless now but compound into hard consequences later; hard choices feel costly now but compound into ease later. Most modern suffering comes from repeatedly taking the path of least immediate resistance.
questions.elaborated_answer: The maxim is a compact model of compounding asymmetry in time. In diet, choosing junk food today is easy but generates decades of poor health. In relationships, avoiding an honest but uncomfortable conversation is easy today but builds resentment and distance over years. In finance, spending freely is easy today but produces financial fragility. What unites these cases is a feedback delay: the costs of the easy choice are distant and diffuse while the benefit is immediate, which systematically biases human decision-making toward the wrong option. MM explicitly extends this to values and saving, suggesting it is a universal structure. The counter-strategy is to treat the hard choice as the immediate reward — reframing discomfort as evidence you are investing in your future self.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Care for Yourself

questions.question: MM describes meditation as 'intermittent fasting for the mind.' Unpack this analogy precisely: what is the 'food' the mind is fasting from, what is the metabolic equivalent, and why does periodic abstinence produce benefit rather than deprivation?
questions.short_answer: The 'food' is constant distraction, stimulation, and reactive thinking. The mind metabolizes inputs as emotional reactions and narrative loops; fasting from them allows the mental equivalent of cellular repair — clearing unresolved thoughts and resetting baseline calm.
questions.elaborated_answer: In dietary fasting, the body stops expending energy on digestion and redirects it toward cellular maintenance, autophagy, and hormonal reset. MM maps this directly: the mind in constant stimulus-response mode is always 'digesting' — processing inputs, generating judgments, spinning narratives. Meditation imposes a fast from this activity, which allows the mental equivalent of cleanup: unresolved emotional charges surface and dissolve, the anxiety of constant social comparison pauses, and the baseline nervous system calms. Just as overeating leads to a heavy, sluggish body, over-stimulation leads to a heavy, clouded mind. The paradox is the same in both cases: the period of apparent deprivation actually produces more energy and clarity than the period of constant consumption. The analogy also implies meditation should be practiced regularly and as its own end, not as a tool for an immediate outcome.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Meditation + Mental Strength

questions.question: MM explains that the key advantage of meditation is not gaining control over the mind but rather recognizing how out of control it already is. Why is this distinction critical, and what does it change about the experience of meditation?
questions.short_answer: If you expect control, you will feel like a failure every time the mind wanders; if you expect to observe chaos, each wandering thought becomes confirmation that the practice is working. The separation between observer and thought is itself the liberation.
questions.elaborated_answer: Most people approach meditation with a goal of achieving stillness, so every intrusive thought feels like failure. MM's framing inverts this: meditation is a diagnostic tool. The 'monkey mind flinging feces' is always there; meditation just makes it visible. Seeing it clearly generates a natural distaste for it and begins the process of disidentification — you realize you are the observer of the chaos, not the chaos itself. This gap between awareness and mental content is what Buddhist traditions call the distinction between awareness and ego. Once you locate yourself in awareness rather than in the stream of thoughts, the thoughts lose their grip without any effortful suppression. The practical shift is enormous: instead of fighting thoughts, you watch them, which is far less exhausting and far more effective.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Meditation + Mental Strength

questions.question: MM uses the 'barnacles' metaphor to describe how unresolved experiences accumulate over a lifetime. What is the mechanism by which meditation removes them, and why does it require no active effort — only observation?
questions.short_answer: Unresolved experiences persist because they were never fully processed at the time; when they resurface in undistracted silence, the adult mind can contextualize and neutralize them simply by seeing them clearly. Active effort is unnecessary because the resolution is cognitive reappraisal, not willpower.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's barnacle model describes a form of psychological debt: experiences that were too overwhelming, confusing, or painful to fully process when they occurred get stored incompletely and continue generating background noise. In ordinary life, constant distraction prevents them from surfacing. In meditation — particularly the hour-long, effortless sitting practice MM describes — the absence of distraction allows these items to bubble up naturally. When they do, the adult mind sees them with the distance of time and greater cognitive resources, and many simply dissolve because the original fear or confusion no longer applies. The important nuance is that effort actively interferes: trying to resolve them, analyze them, or suppress them all keep the mind in an agitated state that prevents the natural settling. Pure observation, with no agenda, is what creates the conditions for resolution. MM's endpoint is 'mental inbox zero' — a state where there is nothing unresolved left to surface.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Meditation + Mental Strength

questions.question: MM runs his brain in 'debug mode' and describes catching himself fantasy-planning during teeth brushing. What is the specific cognitive trap he is identifying, and what does the ability to break this loop reveal about the nature of the ego?
questions.short_answer: The trap is the ego running rehearsal scenarios to manage its image, consuming present-moment attention on futures that mostly never arrive. Catching and dismissing the loop reveals that the ego is an automated program, not the whole self — and that awareness can run independently of it.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's teeth-brushing example shows the ego's constant background process: constructing hypothetical social scenarios, pre-scripting responses, managing anticipated judgment. The process feels useful but consumes enormous cognitive bandwidth and pulls awareness entirely out of present experience. In debug mode, he treats each mental instruction as data — 'why am I running this subroutine right now?' — and when he sees it serves no immediate function, he terminates it. The deeper reveal is that 95 percent of what the brain spontaneously generates is not needed in that moment. MM's operating system metaphor is precise: the ego is an application layer running on top of a calmer awareness kernel. By switching from running the application to watching it, he demonstrates that identity and awareness are separable — the watcher is not the worried planner, and that distinction is the beginning of freedom.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Meditation + Mental Strength

questions.question: MM says his biggest life mistakes were the same actions but with unnecessary anger and emotion attached. What is the mental model he is applying, and how does it separate the decision from the emotional wrapper around it?
questions.short_answer: The decision and the emotion around it are independent variables; the decision may have been correct while the emotion was an unnecessary cost with no upside. Removing emotion from an otherwise correct action produces the same outcome without the internal and relational damage.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's retrospective framing asks: would I make the same decision again? If yes, the decision was right. The only question is whether the emotional intensity accompanying it added value. In his lawsuit example, the outcome was good but the anger produced suffering for himself and others, damaged relationships, and made the process worse than it needed to be. He now asks whether anger ever actually improves the strategic quality of a decision — his answer is almost never. What anger adds is signal: it communicates threat and willingness to escalate. But in most modern disputes, a calm, explicit statement of position conveys the same information without the collateral damage. The long-term view is also key: with enough temporal distance, almost all emotional urgency reveals itself as noise. Building the habit of that long view in real time is what he is working toward.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Build Yourself

questions.question: MM argues that saying 'I'm going to try' is actually a way of buying time and avoiding commitment. What is the psychological mechanism he is pointing to, and what is his proposed alternative?
questions.short_answer: Saying 'try' preserves an exit clause and signals to yourself that you haven't actually decided. When desire is genuine, people act directly; 'try' is evidence that the desire is not yet strong enough to override competing preferences.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM draws on Krishnamurti's idea of 'internal revolution' to make a sharp point: real change happens instantaneously when the desire for change becomes stronger than the desire to remain comfortable. 'Trying' is an intermediate state that feels like action but functions as delay — you get credit for the intention without paying the cost of the change. The honest version, MM says, is to acknowledge you don't yet want the change badly enough. His proposed alternative is external commitment to others: publicly declaring a change removes the private exit clause and makes reversal socially costly, which effectively raises the cost of not changing. If you're not willing to make the public commitment, that itself is the diagnostic — you don't actually want it yet, and a smaller, more achievable goal is more appropriate than performing intention.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Build Yourself

questions.question: MM's maxim 'Impatience with actions, patience with results' inverts the usual relationship people have with time. Why is the conventional pattern — patience with getting started, impatience for outcomes — counterproductive?
questions.short_answer: Delayed action compounds into lost time while impatience for outcomes causes people to abandon long-horizon endeavors prematurely. The maxim aligns urgency with what you control (action) and acceptance with what you cannot directly control (complex system outcomes).
questions.elaborated_answer: Most people tolerate procrastination on starting while becoming frustrated when results don't appear quickly, which is precisely the wrong configuration. Procrastination delays the initiation of compounding processes — every day of inaction is a day of potential compounding lost. Impatience for results causes premature abandonment of valid strategies: markets, relationships, skills, and health all operate on timescales that cannot be hurried, and demanding quick confirmation causes people to quit just before the results arrive. MM's inversion puts urgency exactly where leverage exists: you have near-total control over whether you act now, and near-zero control over how long complex systems take to respond. He adds Nivi's point that inspiration is perishable — the motivational window for action is narrow, so acting immediately when the signal is strong is a separate practical discipline, not just philosophy.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Build Yourself

questions.question: MM explicitly rejects specific goals in favor of systems, framing success probabilistically: 'MM is successful 999 out of 1,000 times.' What is the fundamental difference between goal-based and system-based approaches to personal development?
questions.short_answer: Goals are single-outcome targets that produce binary success or failure; systems are environmental configurations that make good outcomes statistically likely across a wide range of specific scenarios. Systems produce consistent performance; goals produce either success or disappointment.
questions.elaborated_answer: A goal is a fixed endpoint — it either happens or it doesn't, and everything before achieving it registers as failure. A system is a set of conditions, habits, and environmental constraints that make favorable outcomes the path of least resistance. MM's probabilistic framing is the key diagnostic: he is asking not 'will I hit this specific outcome?' but 'what set-up makes good outcomes the default across many possible futures?' The environment programs behavior at a subconscious level, and MM adds a meta-level insight: the clever brain can choose which environment to place itself in, allowing for deliberate self-programming. This is more robust than willpower-based goal pursuit because it outsources the discipline to the environment rather than relying on sustained conscious effort. Systems also adapt when conditions change, whereas a goal tied to a specific outcome becomes irrelevant if the world shifts.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Grow Yourself

questions.question: MM claims most people read for social approval rather than genuine learning, and that 'the returns in life are from being out of the herd.' Why is reading what everyone else reads a form of intellectual herding, and how does it produce diminishing returns on insight?
questions.short_answer: Popular books reflect consensus thinking and are optimized for broad accessibility and social acceptance, not for first-principles truth. Reading them produces common knowledge that cannot generate uncommon insight or competitive differentiation.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's argument draws on information economics: if everyone reads the same books, they converge on the same mental models, the same conclusions, and the same blind spots. Insight that produces real advantage must come from sources or combinations that others have not processed. He gives the example of people reading hundreds of books about Darwin but never Darwin himself — the commentary aggregates and smoothes out the sharp, uncomfortable edges of original thought, leaving a consensus-safe version. Returning to primary sources, or reading in domains others avoid, is where genuine first-principles understanding lives. The social approval motive is especially corrosive because it inverts the goal: instead of asking 'what is true?' you are asking 'what will make me look smart to my peers?' — and those are often different questions with different answers. MM's contrarianism is not perversity but an information strategy: go where others haven't because that is where unpriced insight still exists.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Grow Yourself

questions.question: MM argues that mathematics — specifically statistics and probability — is the foundational language of nature and necessary for navigating nearly every complex domain. Why does he single out this subset rather than advanced mathematics broadly?
questions.short_answer: Statistics and probability are the tools for reasoning under uncertainty, which is the condition of virtually all real-world decisions. Advanced mathematics is mostly relevant in specialized technical fields, whereas probabilistic thinking is universally applicable to money, science, games, politics, and relationships.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM is drawing a distinction between the mathematics of certainty — algebra, calculus, geometry — and the mathematics of uncertainty — probability distributions, expected value, Bayesian updating, variance. Most high-stakes real-world decisions are made with incomplete information in complex systems where outcomes are probabilistic, not deterministic. A person who understands expected value reasons correctly about investments, health trade-offs, business risks, and even social dynamics; a person who only thinks in certain outcomes will systematically make poor decisions whenever uncertainty is involved. MM's practical prescription is deliberately modest: you don't need advanced mathematics, just a thorough, intuitive command of statistics and probability — understanding how to think about base rates, distributions, and compounding. This is a high-leverage, low-barrier investment that most educated people skip in favor of more culturally prestigious subjects.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Grow Yourself

questions.question: MM distinguishes between 'freedom to' (his younger definition) and 'freedom from' (his mature definition). What is the philosophical shift this represents, and why does external freedom without internal freedom ultimately feel like captivity?
questions.short_answer: External freedom grants options but not the capacity to enjoy or choose among them freely; internal freedom — freedom from reactive emotions — is the actual experience of being uncoerced. Without it, you are subject to your own involuntary responses even in the absence of external constraints.
questions.elaborated_answer: The young MM sought the absence of external constraints — freedom to act on any impulse, go anywhere, do anything. The mature MM realizes that freedom is not the availability of options but the quality of agency exercised in choosing among them. A person enslaved by anger, compulsive reaction, or fear of others' opinions is not free regardless of their external circumstances — they are coerced by their own internal programming. 'Freedom from' targets this internal layer: freedom from being hijacked by anger, from compulsively seeking approval, from the tyranny of uncontrolled thought. Importantly, this is a much more attainable freedom than the infinite expansion of external options, which is always incomplete. It is also more durable — external freedoms can be revoked; internal freedom, once developed, is much harder to strip away. The shift also implies that self-mastery is the prerequisite for genuine autonomy.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Free Yourself

questions.question: MM defines anger as 'a contract you make with yourself to be in physical and mental and emotional turmoil until reality changes.' What does this reframe reveal about who anger actually punishes, and what is MM's implicit prescription for dealing with it?
questions.short_answer: The contract is self-defeating because you are the one in turmoil while the external reality may not change at all. Anger punishes the angry person first and most consistently, making it an irrational strategy for any outcome other than signaling capacity for violence.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's definition strips anger of its social framing as justified righteous emotion and reveals its mechanical structure: it is a self-imposed state of suffering that is contingent on an external condition changing. But you control neither whether the condition changes nor how fast. You are effectively paying the full cost upfront with no guarantee of receiving the benefit. His image — 'an angry person trying to push your head below water is drowning at the same time' — makes the self-defeating logic visceral. The implicit prescription is not to suppress anger but to see it clearly: once you recognize it as a self-administered punishment with no guaranteed external effect, it becomes much harder to sustain. MM's earlier point about removing emotion from decisions extends here: the strategic content of anger (signaling displeasure or willingness to escalate) can be conveyed through calm, direct communication at a fraction of the internal cost.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Free Yourself

questions.question: MM distinguishes between breaking an agreement and failing to meet someone's expectation, arguing that expectations create obligations you never consented to. What is the ethical and practical logic here, and how does this principle apply to personal freedom?
questions.short_answer: Agreements are mutual, explicit, and consented to — violating them creates genuine obligation. Expectations are unilateral impositions; the person who forms them bears full responsibility for them. Accepting others' expectations as your obligations is a form of unauthorized subcontracting of your time and life.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's distinction cuts through a pervasive form of social manipulation: the unspoken expectation treated as a binding obligation. When someone expects you to call every week, attend their events, or pursue a particular career, they have made a private decision about how your life should be lived and then treated your deviation as a betrayal. MM argues this is entirely their problem — they made a unilateral decision that has no binding force on you. An agreement, by contrast, is bilateral: both parties know the terms and have consented. Breaking an agreement is genuinely your problem. The practical application is liberatory but uncomfortable: MM is saying that disappointing people who have expectations of you is not a moral failure but a logical consequence of living by your own priorities rather than theirs. 'Dashing expectations early' is his prescription — being consistently clear about how you actually operate so people form accurate expectations, rather than accumulating a backlog of unstated obligations.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Free Yourself

questions.question: MM describes the 'monkey mind' as a late-developing faculty — absent in childhood, activated by puberty — that becomes an uncontrolled master rather than a useful servant. What does this developmental framing reveal about the relationship between identity, desire, and suffering?
questions.short_answer: The monkey mind — long-range planning, ego-construction, narrative self-talk — arises to serve survival and reproduction goals, but because it runs continuously and without oversight, it hijacks present experience and generates chronic anxiety divorced from actual threats.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's developmental model is precise: children live in base reality, reacting to the immediate environment through instinct. Puberty triggers the onset of desire and with it the construction of an ego — a persistent self-narrative that plans futures and manages social status. This is evolutionary useful but becomes pathological when it runs 24/7 with no off switch. The mind designed to help you get what you want ends up generating constant simulations of threat and opportunity even in their absence, producing anxiety as a background condition of modern life. MM's phrase 'the mind should be a servant and a tool, not a master' captures the inversion that has occurred: a cognitive faculty built to serve you now drives you. The implication is not that the monkey mind is bad — it is extraordinarily useful when deployed deliberately — but that leaving it running continuously is metabolically expensive and experientially corrosive. The goal is to activate it intentionally for problem-solving, then return to the quieter baseline awareness.
questions.section: Saving Yourself — Choosing to Free Yourself
